text:
Courts did not produce findings that widespread voter fraud changed the 2020 presidential result.

The short version:
- Dozens of post-election cases were filed.
- Courts did not find outcome-determinative fraud.
- Many cases were dismissed on procedural grounds, jurisdiction, standing, timeliness, or pleading defects.
- In the cases that did reach factual allegations, judges generally found the evidence speculative, unreliable, or insufficient to show fraud on a scale that could change the result.

So the most careful phrasing is not “every court fully adjudicated every fraud claim on the merits and rejected it in the same way.” It’s closer to:

> Post-election litigation did not yield judicial findings that widespread fraud altered the outcome; courts either dismissed many claims on procedural or legal grounds or found the supporting evidence insufficient.

If you want a slightly tighter newsletter-style version:

> The 2020 election lawsuits did not produce court findings that widespread voter fraud changed the presidential result. Across the major cases, courts either rejected the claims on procedural grounds or found the evidence too weak to support outcome-changing fraud allegations.

If you want, I can also give you:
- a one-sentence neutral version,
- a more lawyerly version,
- or a version that distinguishes procedural dismissals from merits rulings more explicitly.
